steps.text: This comment sets our expectations. We're building a tiny language model, and all it does is predict a likely next word. It doesn't understand anything.
We import the random module so we can make random choices. Then we seed it with the number four, which means the randomness is repeatable and everyone gets the same output.
Here's our corpus, the text the model learns from. It's a triple-quoted string spanning several lines, packed with short sentences about a model, a lawyer, and some fake cases.
We call split on the corpus, which breaks that big string into a list of individual words wherever there's whitespace. Now we can work word by word.
This comment explains our plan: for every word, record which words follow it. We start with an empty dictionary called next words to hold that table.
We zip the words list with itself shifted by one, giving us each word paired with the word right after it. For every pair, we grab the list for word a and append word b onto it.
This comment says our generate step picks a plausible next word each time. We define a function generate that takes a starting word and n, which defaults to eighteen words.
We set up two variables: w holds our current word, and out is our output list, already containing that first word so it shows up in the result.

We loop n times. Each round we ask the table for words that followed our current word, then randomly pick one. If the word isn't found, we fall back to a period, and we append the choice to our output.
Finally we join all the words in out with spaces between them, turning our list back into a readable sentence to return.
Now we test it. We print what the model says starting from the word the, and again starting from plausible, so we can compare two different generated sentences.
These comments drive home the big idea. The model never lies or tells the truth on purpose. It only continues text plausibly, just like the huge models, minus the scale.
The first challenge invites you to remove the seed and run it a few times. Same corpus, but you'll get different confident nonsense every single run.
The second challenge suggests adding a sentence of your own real facts to the corpus, then watching the model remix them into things you never actually said.
This comment tells us the whole idea. We're building a checker that looks at a prompt and asks: does it have the four parts of a good prompt?
Here's our first clue list, role signs. These are little phrases like 'you are' or 'act as' that hint the prompt is giving the AI a role to play.
Context signs are phrases that suggest you gave the AI something to work with, like 'here is' or 'my data'. Notice this list simply continues onto the next line.
Format signs are words asking for a specific shape of answer, like 'bullets', 'table', or 'summary'. If any of these appear, the prompt is describing the output format.
Constraint signs are limiting words like 'only', 'must', or 'at most'. These show the prompt is setting rules and boundaries for the answer.
Now we define a function called check that takes a prompt. First we make a lowercase copy called p, so our matching ignores capital letters.
We set up two things: parts, an empty list to collect our findings, and score, starting at zero to count how many of the four parts we find.
This loop walks through all four categories at once, pairing each name with its list of signs. So each turn we handle role, then context, then format, then constraints.
If any sign phrase appears in the prompt, we found that part. We add one to the score and record a plus with the part's name to celebrate the win.
Otherwise, none of the signs matched, so this part is missing. We add a minus and mark it MISSING, so the person can see what to add.
Now we turn the score into a friendly label. A score of zero says WISH, and it climbs up to RESEARCH-GRADE at four, using the score as an index into this list.
We print the label neatly right-aligned in fourteen spaces, followed by the first fifty-eight characters of the prompt and some dots, so long prompts don't flood the screen.
Then we loop through our parts list and print each finding, indented under the label, so you get a tidy checklist of what's present and what's missing.
Time to test it. This first call, 'Explain AI', is bare bones with none of the four parts, so watch it score low.
This second prompt asks to summarize a paper and pastes an abstract. It has context and a format word, so it should score a bit higher.
This third prompt is the full package: it gives a role, provides context, asks for three bullets, and demands exact quotes. These three lines join into one string, and it should hit the top label.
Here's your first challenge: write tonight's five homework prompts and run each through the checker to see how they score.
And a thinking challenge: this checker only spots keywords, so it can be tricked by stuffing in the right words. Ask yourself what that reminds you of in real life.
